coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of biogenic CDs integrated TiO2 nano-heterojunction for the assessment of visible light mediated photocatalytic dye degradation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Smrithi, S.P.
Devendra, Bharath K.
Sinchana, K.P.
Dharshan, K.
Sneha,
Kottam, Nagaraju
Prasannakumar, S.G.
Sanjana, R.
Rakshitha Bai, K.
coredata.dc:description: Modification of titania nanophotocatalysts is well-recognized for their efficacy in photocatalysis under UV irradiation, particularly in organic dye degradation applications. This research focuses on the structural modification of TiO2 nanoparticles through the integration of biogenic carbon dots (CDs) to tailor its optical characteristics and thereby to redefine its photocatalytic functionalities. CDs are carbon nanoparticles with dimensions less than 10 nm adorned with organic functional groups which exhibit distinctive optical and electronic properties, rendering them versatile for numerous applications. In this study, carbon dots were synthesized from Mentha piperita (peppermint) using a hydrothermal method. The resulting TiO2 and CDs@TiO2 nanomaterials underwent thorough characterization via XRD, FTIR, UV-DRS spectroscopy, SEM, and HRTEM techniques. Crystal violet dye degradation studies were conducted, revealing enhanced degradation efficiency (97 %) for CDs@TiO2 in comparison to TiO2 (67 %). The kinetic study revealed that the degradation process fits well with the pseudo second order kinetics. This investigation underscores the pivotal role of carbon dots in augmenting photocatalytic efficiency by mitigating charge carrier recombination, attributed to their electron acceptor-donor characteristics. Additionally, the study portrays the potential of carbon dots derived from eco-friendly sources, such as pepper mint, to serve as a supportive element in the design and advancement of an efficient visible light-based photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2
CDs
Dye degradation
Photo catalysis
Waste water treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of TiO2 and CDs@TiO2 nanoparticles
Photocatalytic dye degradation activities of TiO2 and CDs@TiO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern obtained for TiO2 and CDs@TiO2
(a) SEM image of CDs@TiO2 (b) EDX spectrum of CDs@TiO2
Images of HRTEM of (a) CDs (b) CDs@TiO2
(a) UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CDs (b) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs (c) FTIR spectrum for CDs.

UV–Visible absorbance spectra of crystal violet dye obtained during different time intervals using (a) TiO2 and (b) CDs@TiO2 as photocatalysts (c) Images of crystal violet dye samples withdrawn at regular intervals of time during the photodegradation.

(a) Optimization of CDs@TiO2 dosage for dye degradation (b) C/C0 values against time for different dye concentrations (c) Percentage of dye degradation against various concentrations of crystal violet dye.

(a) Radical trapping experiment for CDs@TiO2 dosage in CV dye degradation (b) UCPL spectra for CDs excited at higher wavelengths (c) Stability study of the photocatalyst, CDs@TiO2 against the degradation of crystal violet dye.
Photodegradation kinetics of CDs@TiO2 nanocomposite against crystal violet dye (a) pseudo-first order rate and (b) pseudo-second order rate.
Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation for CV dye using CDs@TiO2.
Comparison of the as synthesized CDs@TiO2 towards dye degradation with the literature reports.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of biogenic CDs integrated TiO2 nano-heterojunction for the assessment of visible light mediated photocatalytic dye degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modification of titania nanophotocatalysts is well-recognized for their efficacy in photocatalysis under UV irradiation, particularly in organic dye degradation applications. This research focuses on the structural modification of TiO2 nanoparticles through the integration of biogenic carbon dots (CDs) to tailor its optical characteristics and thereby to redefine its photocatalytic functionalities. CDs are carbon nanoparticles with dimensions less than 10 nm adorned with organic functional groups which exhibit distinctive optical and electronic properties, rendering them versatile for numerous applications. In this study, carbon dots were synthesized from Mentha piperita (peppermint) using a hydrothermal method. The resulting TiO2 and CDs@TiO2 nanomaterials underwent thorough characterization via XRD, FTIR, UV-DRS spectroscopy, SEM, and HRTEM techniques. Crystal violet dye degradation studies were conducted, revealing enhanced degradation efficiency (97 %) for CDs@TiO2 in comparison to TiO2 (67 %). The kinetic study revealed that the degradation process fits well with the pseudo second order kinetics. This investigation underscores the pivotal role of carbon dots in augmenting photocatalytic efficiency by mitigating charge carrier recombination, attributed to their electron acceptor-donor characteristics. Additionally, the study portrays the potential of carbon dots derived from eco-friendly sources, such as pepper mint, to serve as a supportive element in the design and advancement of an efficient visible light-based photocatalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The very beneficial lifestyle that humans have evolved has greatly polluted the universe. One of the most persistent forms of environmental resistance in the modern era is the presence of non-biodegradable organic materials such as industrial colors and heavy metal ions in natural reservoirs as a result of massive industrial waste chemical water being dumped into them. The process of removing the pollutants from contaminated water always progresses (Sum et al., 2018; Hamoud et al., 2017) and egresses provide significant challenges to the disposal of municipal solid trash. Therefore, using kitchen trash as a resource opens up a new world of extremely substantial down with cutting-edge green waste water technology (Xu et al., 2015; Thomas and Kottam, 2013).

Heterogeneous photo catalyst has transfixed appreciable consideration in the past decennium as a result of their broad functionality and enhanced execution. Amongst the photocatalysts, anatase titanium dioxide (TiO2) has displayed to be propitious; given that its efficaciously oxidizing power, good photo longevity, eco-friendly, chemical inertness, and is affordable (Verma et al., 2012; Mahanthappa et al., 2019; Shamsipur et al., 2017). In spite of their considerable performances and perdurability, TiO2-based photocatalyst have defined applications owing to the fact of its wide band gap energy (3.2 eV) and high re-assimilation rate (Bharathi et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2019; Visa et al., 2015; Sailaja Prasannakumaran Nair et al., 2020).

A versatile nanomaterial with remarkable physicochemical properties, including aqueous solubility, photo-induced electron transfer ability, and intrinsic biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) have made a revolution in nanoscience (Che et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2016; Smrithi et al., 2020). This feature combined with CDs has sparked extensive research in all areas of potential applications, including biomedicine, sensor technology, photocatalysis, and optoelectronics (Devendra et al., 2022a,b). The abundance of readily available raw “green” precursors, along with simple methods of simulated advancements, makes them promising nanomaterials for the twenty-first century (M. Wang et al., 2019). Due to its ability to increase the parent semiconductor's photocatalytic capacity, to which it is attached by inhibiting charge carrier recombination, CD can function as an electron harvester and/or converter (Smrithi et al., 2024).

Integrating CDs to TiO2 can offer several advantages. This can result in the broadening of the absorbance spectrum of the photocatalyst, making it more efficient under natural sunlight. CDs act as electron acceptors, reducing the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs generated during photocatalysis (Prashanth et al., 2024). This can prolong the lifetime of charge carriers and may increase the photocatalytic efficiency. The incorporation of CDs enhances the catalytic activity by creating additional active sites on the TiO₂ surface, leading to a higher generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals, which are crucial for dye degradation (Sharieff et al., 2023). The introduction of CDs can increase the hydrophilicity of TiO₂ surfaces, improving the interaction between the photocatalyst and aqueous solutions, which is crucial for effective dye degradation in water treatment. Further, CDs are usually derived from inexpensive and eco-friendly carbon sources, making the integration with TiO₂ a cost-effective and environmentally sustainable solution for photocatalytic dye degradation (Smrithi et al., 2023).

In this current research, CDs are produced using medicinal plant, peppermint as a precursor. XRD, HRTEM, FESEM-EDX and FTIR are the advanced analytical techniques used to characterize CDs and CDs@TiO2. Additionally, the photodegradation of the produced CDs@TiO2 in response to visible light was investigated for the deterioration of the crystal violet dye.

Mentha piperita (peppermint) is obtained from a botanical garden from Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala. Titanium isopropoxide, nitric acid, ethanol and crystal violet dye were procured from Sisco research laboratories private limited, India. All throughout preparation and experiments, double-distilled water has been employed.

To obtain the extract, the peel of peppermint was carefully cleaned and finely ground without the use of any additional chemicals. A Teflon-lined autoclave containing 50 mL of the extract was heated to 180 °C for 12 h. A 0.2 mm microporous membrane was used to filter the resulting brown-colored solution, and its progress was monitored through repeated washings with distilled water and ethanol. Furthermore, packing into a dialysis bag (Mw = 3000) and dialysis for up to 24 h against double-distilled water achieved purification. Eventually, the powdered sample was protected from vacuum drying at 60 °C for an entire night (Zhu et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TiO2 and CDs@TiO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 is obtained by co-precipitation method from Titanium isopropoxide where con. HNO3 served as the precipitating agent. The obtained product was magnetically stirred for 1 h followed by centrifugation using ethanol and DI water. The product was then calcined at 500 °C for 4 h. The same process was repeated by the addition of a known amount (5 wt %) of CDs prior to the precipitation reaction to prepare CDs@TiO2.

With a Bruker D8 advance diffractometer outfitted with Cu k alpha emission with a wavelength of 0. 15406 nm, X-ray diffraction tests were performed. The PerkinElmer Spectrum 1000 was used for the spectral methods of infrared light using the total reflectance attenuated mode. Data from MIRA 3's field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and Quantax 200's energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy studies (EDS) provide the information on morphology and elemental composition. With double detection and adjustable spectral resolutions, the Specord 210 Plus was used for UV–visible absorption recordings.

The photodegradation investigations were carried out using a light source made up of mercury vapor lamp (125 W) in a photocatalytic reactor. Typically, a known amount of photocatalyst was put to the dye solution of known concentration and magnetically agitated for around 30 min to let adsorption-desorption equilibrium to be attained among the pollutant dye and the photocatalyst in the dark conditions. Solution was then continuously stirred while being exposed to visible light. By continuously noting the absorbance maxima at 591 nm after 2 mL of material was removed periodically without disrupting the apparatus, the absorption of CV dye was analyzed.

The diffraction studies as shown in Fig. 1 , TiO2 display the identical diffraction peaks allocated to anatase TiO2 [JCPDS: 21–1272]. Characteristic diffraction signals from CDs are identified in the composite despite the modest amount and high dissemination of CDs in the composite, CDs@TiO2. Additionally, it can also be inferred that the inclusion of CDs into the TiO2 matrix has not altered the phase of parent TiO2 nanostructure.

The CDs are found to be evenly distributed on TiO2 nanoparticles with a steady and impending association, forming film-like CDs@TiO2 heterojunctions, according to the composite's SEM images (Fig. 2 (a) ). The presence of Ti, C, N, and O elements as the primary chemical constituents of the complex are clearly visible in the EDS element mapping over the entire region, citing that the CDs are uniformly allocated on the TiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 2(b)) (Dong et al., 2014).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of CDs and CD@TiO2 have been analyzed. The scrutiny of Fig. 3 . (a) suggest that CDs appear in a size range of 5–10 nm with a uniform spherical morphology. According to Fig. 3 (b), the average diameter of CDs@TiO2 is in 15–20 nm size range with slight agglomeration. The discrete ring pattern in the CDs@TiO2 selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern indicates that the synthesized nanomaterial is crystalline in nature possessing a distinct atomic structure (Devendra et al., 2022a,b).

Being confirmed the size of CDs using HRTEM, the formation of CDs was confirmed using additional characterization methods such as UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence (PL) spectroscopy and FTIR spectroscopy. The UV–Visible absorption spectral peaks affirm the existence of n-π∗ transitions from –CO and π-π∗ transitions from –CC (Fig. 4 (a) ). The broad absorption band clearly shows the capability of CDs to absorb wavelengths initiating from UV region while extending well beyond 400 nm. The appearance of the absorbance spectra is in well agreement with the previous reports on the CDs (Smrithi et al., 2022a,b). In order to investigate the luminescence property, fluorescence spectroscopic studies have been performed and shown in Fig. 4(b). The study revealed the characteristic excitation-dependent emission behavior of CDs owing to its surface decorations; where the maximum emission is observed at 380 nm excitation. CDs excited beyond 380 nm wavelength resulted in a PL emission of less intensity associated with a blue shift. All these optical character related observations further confirm the formation nano-CDs. To find out the different functional groups present and the compositions of the carbon dots, fourier transform infra-red spectroscopy was employed (see Fig. 4 (c)). The symmetric stretching vibrational bands of O–H and N–H are observed at around 3435-3100 cm−1. Also the typical carbonyl stretching is observed at around 1640 cm−1. The narrow band depicting the –CH vibrations are identified at around 660 cm−1 (Kallappa et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic dye degradation activities of TiO2 and CDs@TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation ability of the synthesized TiO2 and CDs@TiO2 was assessed using crystal violet dye as a model target pollutant. It is cationic in nature and finds immense application in industries such as textiles, paper, leather, fertilizers and inkjet printers. A known concentration (10 ppm) of crystal violet dye solution was stirred for uniform concentration, to which 50 mg of the photocatalyst is added and degradation experiments are carried out. The results are depicted in Fig. 5 where bare TiO2 and CDs@TiO2 were compared for their photocatalytic ability. Fig. 5 (a) and (b) depicts the UV–Visible absorbance spectra of crystal violet dye at different time intervals using TiO2 and CDs@TiO2 as photocatalysts respectively where as Fig. 5(c) depicts the images of water sample withdrawn at regular interval of time during the degradation process.

It is interesting to find that the photocatalytic efficiency obtained for TiO2 under visible light illumination is around 67 % where the CDs conjugated with TiO2 evinced a photocatalytic activity of 97.3 % at time duration of 105 min. The obtained results can be attributed to the up-converted photoluminescence property of bio-derived CDs augmented with its well-known electron mediator characteristics. In order to optimize the catalytic dosage, the experiment was repeated with 10 mg, 25 mg and 70 mg dosage of CDs@TiO2 and a dye degradation efficiency of 92.7 %, 94.8 % and 96.6 % respectively was obtained (See Fig. 6 (a) ). This observation implied that 50 mg catalytic dosage was the optimal dosage required to degrade CV dye of 10 ppm concentration. Further, with the 50 mg photocatalytic dosage, photodegradation of CV dye was carried out for different concentrations such as 10 ppm, 20 ppm, 30 ppm and 40 ppm). The results as evident from Fig. 6 (b) and (c) evince a slight decrease in the percentage of dye degradation with increase in concentration of the target pollutant.

Radical trapping experiment was also conducted to understand the mechanism involved in the degradation process using 0.1 mM benzoquinone, 1 mM isopropyl alcohol and 1 mM ammonium oxalate which can act as scavengers for holes, hydroxyl radical and superoxide anion radical respectively. It was found that the addition of ammonium oxalate reduced the degradation process considerably showing the prominent role played by superoxide anion radical in the degradation process (Fig. 7 (a) ). In order to study the effect of visible light in the photoluminescence properties of CDs, the PL measurements were carried out in the visible spectrum (350–500 nm). It is evident that the intensity of peaks started to diminish above 400 nm excitation which shows the reduced recombination of charge carriers and also the emission wavelength is shifted towards lower wavelength, UV region which is called as upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) that can further result in the excitation of TiO2 nanoparticles Fig. 7(b). In order to understand the stability and reusability of the synthesized CDs@TiO2 photocatalyst, the catalyst was regenerated by ultrasonication followed by centrifugation and the degradation experiment was repeated (Devendra et al., 2021). It was found that up to three cycles the degradation was not affected adversely. But the percentage of degradation was found to be slightly decreased from fourth cycle onwards and the results are shown in Fig. 7 (c).

The kinetics of dye degradation has been examined using Lagergren Pseudo-first order reaction and is obtained by plotting -ln (A/A0) against t. The results corresponding to the pseudo first order kinetics are depicted in Fig. 8 (a) . It is inferred that the catalytic activity of CDs@TiO2 is supported by a regression coefficient of R2 = 0.9344 with K1 corresponding to a value of 0.2755 min−1. Fig. 8 (b) represents the pseudo second order kinetics plot for the degradation of crystal violet dye at optimum conditions which reveals a regression coefficient of R2 = 0.9998 with K2 corresponding to a value of 0.2755 M−1min−1.

For carbon dots, the up-conversion fluorescence (UCPL) properties are already well studied and this work exploited its dual role as a fluorescence emitter as well as electron mediator, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . In the CDs@TiO2 nanohybrid, CDs serve two critical functions to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Primarily, the core structure of CDs endowed with –CC- and the peripheral functional groups like –CO, –NH2 etc. make them absorb visible light and exhibit fluorescence phenomenon associated with lower wavelength emission (UCPL). Secondly, CDs can also act as an electron reservoir, trapping electrons emitted from TiO2 nanoparticles when exposed to sunlight, thereby reducing the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. Additionally, CDs serve as a dispersing support, controlling the morphology of TiO2 in the nanohybrid and preventing the agglomeration of synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles. CDs have a high surface area, and their small size allows them to provide more active sites for adsorption when integrated with TiO₂ nanoparticles (Sun, 2018; Wang et al., 2019b). This can lead to a higher overall surface area for the composite material, enhancing adsorption capacity which can also aid the photodegradation capability since adsorption of the dye molecule onto the catalyst constitutes the primary phase of the whole process. When CDs are loaded onto the surface of titanium dioxide (TiO2), their band edge alignment facilitates the transfer of electrons from the TiO2 surface. This process allows for charge separation, stabilization and reduction of recombination rates. Electrons can move freely along the conductive network of CDs, while holes remain near the TiO2. This separation effectively reduces electron-hole recombination, thereby enhancing the reactivity of the photogenerated electrons and holes under visible light exposure. Subsequently, oxygen radicals (O2•−) are formed when electrons in the CD's conductive network combine with adsorbed O2 molecules on the CD surface. When the CDs@TiO2 nanohybrid is exposed to solar light, CDs absorb visible light and emit shorter wavelengths (325–425 nm) due to their up-conversion properties as evident from Fig. 7 (b). This emitted light excites TiO2, generating electron-hole pairs (e−/h+). These pairs react with water (H2O) and oxygen (O2) to produce reactive oxygen species like hydroxyl radicals (OH•) and superoxide anions (O2•−), which subsequently degrade crystal violet dye. The following equations depicts the possible chemical reaction involved in the degradation process
(1) CDs + hν → CDs∗
(2) CDs∗ →CDs + hν’
(3) TiO2 + hν’ → e− (VB) + h+ (CB)
(4) O2 + e− → O2⋅ −
(5) TiO2(e−)+⋅O2 − +H+ → TiO2+HO2 ⋅
(6) HO2⋅+H+ → H2O2
(7) H2O2+e−→ OH⋅+OH−
(8) h+ (OH⋅ or O2⋅−) + CV → Products
A comparison of the works already reported in the literature where the UCPL property of CDs have been exploited to achieve enhanced degradation is shown in Table 1 .

To conclude, this work explored the synthesis of CDs from the medicinal plant, Mentha piperita. The formation of CDs has been confirmed using UV–Visible absorption, photoluminescence, FTIR and HR-TEM analysis. XRD and SEM images of CDs@TiO2 confirmed the presence of CDs in TiO2 matrixes. The degradation studies of crystal violet dye demonstrated a significant improvement in degradation efficiency with CDs@TiO2, achieving 97 %, compared to 67 % with TiO2 alone adhering to pseudo-second order kinetics. This highlights the crucial function of carbon dots in enhancing photocatalytic performance by reducing charge carrier recombination, thanks to their electron acceptor-donor properties. Moreover, the research showcases the promise of carbon dots sourced from eco-friendly materials, like peppermint, as a beneficial component in the development and optimization of effective visible light-driven photocatalysts.

S.P. Smrithi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Bharath K. Devendra: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. K.P. Sinchana: Software, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. K. Dharshan: Software, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sneha: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Nagaraju Kottam: Visualization, Validation, Supervision. S.G. Prasannakumar: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. R. Sanjana: Software, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation. K. Rakshitha Bai: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
